title: The Noticing Game
developmental_frame: At 9–10, thinking is concrete and literal, and learning happens through play, doing, and collecting rather than abstract discussion. Kids this age are natural noticers and sorters — they love grouping, comparing, and asking 'why' — but they can't yet hold a hypothesis or run a fair test the way an older child can. Keep every loop short, hands-on, and finishing the same day, or attention drifts.
authentic_value_meaning: Value at this age is being the one who *noticed* something real that everyone else walked past, and getting to share it. It's not money and not a product — it's 'look what I found out'. The audience is family and friends, and the reward is their genuine interest.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child becomes a 'noticer' — collecting and studying ordinary things (leaves, coins, sounds, bugs) and spotting things grown-ups miss. The pull is the pride of being sharp-eyed and the small delight of surprising an adult with something they noticed. Collecting and sorting are already deeply satisfying at this age, so the activity motivates itself.

artifact: A 'noticing journal' or discovery card: a small collection the child observed and sorted into groups, with one real 'why' question and their best guess at the answer.
success_criteria: The child looked closely enough to describe details they hadn't noticed before.
They sorted their collection into groups and can explain the rule they used.
They asked one real 'why' question and offered a guess.

lessons.title: Look really closely
lessons.concept: Most people glance at things. A noticer *looks* — long enough to see the little details everyone else misses. Slowing down and really looking is the first thinking skill.
lessons.real_example: Look at a single leaf for a whole minute. Suddenly you see tiny lines, little holes, colours changing at the edge — all there the whole time, just unnoticed.
lessons.apply_task: Pick five ordinary things (a coin, a leaf, a shoe, whatever). Look at each for one minute and write or draw one detail you'd never noticed before.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which thing surprised you most when you really looked? Was the detail there all along?
lessons.feedback_task: Show an adult one of your things and share your detail. Ask if they'd ever noticed it. Their surprise is your reward.
lessons.teach_back_task: Play the one-minute looking game with a younger child and help them find a detail.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is a look-with-your-own-eyes game — no screen needed. The whole point is *you* noticing, which a computer can't do for you.
assets_needed: Noticing journal pages (5 slots for detail drawings)
success_criteria: Five never-before-noticed details are recorded from real objects.

lessons.title: Sort it into groups
lessons.concept: Once you've collected things, you can sort them — big/small, smooth/rough, by colour. Sorting is thinking: you're deciding what's the same and what's different, and picking a rule.
lessons.real_example: Tip out a box of buttons and you'll naturally start grouping them — by colour, then by size. Scientists sort the whole world this way, from animals to stars.
lessons.apply_task: Gather a collection (leaves, rocks, toys, coins). Sort it into groups. Write down the rule you used, then re-sort it a totally different way.
lessons.reflection_prompt: You sorted the same things two different ways — so which grouping is 'right'? (Trick question: it depends on what you care about.)
lessons.feedback_task: Show your groups to someone and see if they can guess your sorting rule. If they can't, was your rule clear?
lessons.teach_back_task: Give a friend your collection and challenge them to sort it, then explain their rule to you.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Sort with your own hands and eyes — that's the thinking. No app needed to move real objects into real piles.
success_criteria: A collection was sorted two different ways with each rule stated.

lessons.title: Ask a big 'why?'
lessons.concept: Noticers don't just look — they wonder. Pick one thing you noticed and ask why it's like that. Then make your best guess. A good guess isn't cheating; it's how thinking starts.
lessons.real_example: 'Why are most leaves green?' or 'Why do heavier coins feel colder?' You don't need the final answer yet — asking the question and guessing is the win.
lessons.apply_task: Pick one thing from your collection and write a 'why' question about it. Then write your best guess at the answer.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where did your guess come from — something you already knew? Good guesses usually connect to things you've seen before.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask an adult your 'why' question. Compare their answer to your guess. Were you close?
lessons.teach_back_task: Ask a family member to make up their own 'why' question about something at home, and swap guesses.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Make your *own* guess first, before anyone (or any computer) tells you the answer. The guessing is the exercise; getting handed the answer skips the workout.
success_criteria: One real 'why' question and a reasoned guess are written down.

lessons.title: Share your discovery
lessons.concept: A discovery isn't finished until you share it. Telling someone what you noticed, sorted, and wondered turns you from a looker into a discoverer.
lessons.real_example: Show-and-tell works because sharing a real find is exciting for everyone. 'Look what I noticed' is a great thing to be able to say.
lessons.apply_task: Finish your noticing journal (details + your groups + your why-question and guess) and present it to your family like a real discovery.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to share something *you* noticed? Which part were you proudest of?
lessons.feedback_task: Ask your listeners what they found most interesting. Note it — it tells you what makes a discovery exciting to others.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help someone else in your family start their own noticing journal.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Share it with real people, face to face. The joy here is a real person going 'wow, I never noticed that' — that's a human moment, not a screen one.
success_criteria: The child presented a completed noticing journal to a real audience.
